title: Non-record: BitNet b1.58 — 65M ternary params beat 4-hour baseline in 10 minutes (val_bpb=1.2029)
body: ## BitNet b1.58: 64.5M Ternary Parameters in 15.1MB val_bpb: 1.2029 (post-roundtrip) | 15.11 MB | 8×H100, 10 minutes ### The idea The baseline's 17.1M params are saturated long before the wallclock runs out (T/N ≈ 424×). Chinchilla says: fit more parameters. BitNet b1.58 lets me pack 64.5M ternary {-1, 0, 1} parameters into 15.1MB via base-3 encoding at 1.6 bits/param — 3.8× more params than the baseline in the same artifact size. ### What's different - **BitLinear layers** throughout: ternary weight quantization with STE in every forward pass - **Near-Zero quantization gap** (0.002 BPB): model trains quantized, so the roundtrip is lossless. No post-training quantization fight. - **fp16 scale simulation** during training (.half().float()) ensures the stored scales match what the model saw - **Base-3 packing**: 5 trits per byte, lossless, near the theoretical 1.585 bits/ trit minimum - No sliding window, no eval tricks ### Results | Model | Params | Artifact | val_bpb | Quant gap | Time | |-------|--------|----------|---------|-----------|------| | Current SOTA (INT6+SW) | ~20M | ~15.4MB | 1.1748 | ~0.01 | 10 min | | Naive Baseline | 17.1M | 15.9MB | 1.2244 | 0.007 | 10 min | | 4-Hour Baseline | 17.1M | 15.9MB | 1.2074 | 0.033 | 4 hours | | BitNet b1.58 | 64.5M | 15.1MB | 1.2029 | 0.002 | 10 min | A 10-minute ternary model beats 4 hours of full-precision training. The full write- up with scaling law analysis is in the README.

comments.body:
The “roundtrip” eval reloads from the in-memory `tern_obj` rather than the actual on-disk compressed artifact. This doesn’t validate that the chosen `tern_blob` (lzma/zlib/zstd) can be decompressed and `torch.load`’d correctly. For a true roundtrip check, re-read `final_model.ternary.ptz`, decompress based on `compress_method`, `torch.load` it, and dequantize that object.
```suggestion
    # Re-load ternary artifact from disk to validate compression/decompression roundtrip
    with open("final_model.ternary.ptz", "rb") as f:
        tern_blob_disk = f.read()
    if compress_method == "lzma":
        tern_raw_disk = lzma.decompress(tern_blob_disk)
    elif compress_method == "zlib":
        tern_raw_disk = zlib.decompress(tern_blob_disk)
    elif compress_method == "zstd":
        if not HAS_ZSTD:
            raise RuntimeError("Zstandard compression selected but zstandard is not available at load time.")
        dctx = zstd.ZstdDecompressor()
        tern_raw_disk = dctx.decompress(tern_blob_disk)
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown compression method for ternary artifact: {compress_method}")
    tern_buf_disk = io.BytesIO(tern_raw_disk)
    tern_obj_disk = torch.load(tern_buf_disk, map_location="cpu")

    base_model.load_state_dict(dequantize_state_dict_ternary(tern_obj_disk), strict=True)
```

The markdown tables in this README use `||` at the start of rows, which renders as an extra empty column in most markdown renderers. Replace the double leading pipes with single `|` so the tables render correctly on GitHub.

`BitLinear._quantize_weights()` reshapes `w` with `w.reshape(-1, g)` without padding or validation. If `in_features * out_features` is not divisible by `group_size` (or if you later change model dims / head dims), this will throw at runtime. Add an explicit divisibility check (e.g., in `__init__`) or pad before grouping so the layer works for arbitrary shapes.

In the cached-path export, `quantize_state_dict_ternary(..., group_size=...)` stores `"group_size": group_size` even though the cached `q` was produced by the `BitLinear` module’s own `group_size`. If these ever diverge, dequantization will reshape with the wrong group size and corrupt/throw. Prefer storing `q.shape[1]`/`mod.group_size` in the entry (and optionally assert it matches the function arg).
```suggestion
            # Derive group size from cached q to ensure consistency with BitLinear
            cached_group_size = q.shape[1]
            if cached_group_size != group_size:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Cached BitLinear group_size ({cached_group_size}) does not match "
                    f"quantize_state_dict_ternary argument ({group_size}) for parameter '{name}'."
                )
            packed_bytes, pack_meta = pack_ternary(q)
            quantized[name] = {"type": "ternary", "packed": packed_bytes,
                               "scale": scale, "shape": list(shape),
                               "padded_cols": shape[1], "group_size": cached_group_size,
```

`quantize_state_dict_ternary()` eagerly creates a full `cpu().float()` copy (`t = tensor.detach().cpu().float()...`) before checking `if name in cache`. For cached BitLinear weights this does unnecessary host copies of the largest tensors, slowing export and increasing peak memory. Check the cache first and only materialize `t` for the non-cached paths.
```suggestion
        if name in cache:
            q, scale, shape = cache[name]
            q = q.detach().cpu()
            scale = scale.detach().cpu()
            packed_bytes, pack_meta = pack_ternary(q)
            quantized[name] = {"type": "ternary", "packed": packed_bytes,
                               "scale": scale, "shape": list(shape),
                               "padded_cols": shape[1], "group_size": group_size,
                               "n_trits": pack_meta[0]}
            stats["ternary_bytes"] += len(packed_bytes) + scale.numel() * 2
        else:
            t = tensor.detach().cpu().float().contiguous()
            if t.ndim == 2 and t.numel() > 65_536 and "tok_emb" not in name:
                pad = (group_size - t.shape[1] % group_size) % group_size
                t_padded = F.pad(t, (0, pad)) if pad > 0 else t
                t_grouped = t_padded.reshape(-1, group_size)
                scale = t_grouped.abs().mean(-1, keepdim=True).clamp(min=1e-8).half().float()
                q = (t_grouped / scale).round().clamp(-1, 1).to(torch.int8)
                packed_bytes, pack_meta = pack_ternary(q)
                quantized[name] = {"type": "ternary", "packed": packed_bytes,
                                   "scale": scale.to(torch.float16).squeeze(-1),
                                   "shape": list(t.shape), "padded_cols": t_padded.shape[1],
                                   "group_size": group_size, "n_trits": pack_meta[0]}
                stats["ternary_bytes"] += len(packed_bytes) + scale.numel() * 2
            else:
                quantized[name] = {"type": "fp16", "data": t.to(torch.float16)}
                stats["fp16_bytes"] += t.numel() * 2
```

@0hq Looks like #139 and #367 may have been missed in the queue, neither got reviewed. #139 was the first competitive
ternary/BitNet submission (1.2029 BPB, day 1) and predates #640. Would appreciate a look when you get a chance.

@ksang123 I feel like tagging my PR in this and also leaving a kind-of snarky comment on my own PR as "exactly what I hoped would happen when I submitted 139" is a bit disingenous, almost as if my work extends or builds upon yours. My work started as soon as the challenge was announced, and as I am the creator of the [Bitnet Rust library](https://github.com/CiprianFlorin-Ifrim/bitnet-llm-rs)/[bitnet-llm](https://crates.io/crates/bitnet-llm), with a lot of private work and research done on Bitnets (and general int8 transformers) and their application on low power MCUs, it made sense to work on that for this challenge given the constraints. I simply did not want to submit something to "hold a place in the queue that I could update later" until it was complete, otherwise within 3h on the day of the challenge I could've been under 1.20 and mention how I was first. You can find a proper research document on the circa 250 runs I did here [Results Document](https://github.com/CiprianFlorin-Ifrim/openai-parameter-golf-lab/blob/main/documents/RESULTS.pdf) (formatted by Claude from my [RESULTS.md](https://github.com/CiprianFlorin-Ifrim/openai-parameter-golf-lab/blob/main/RESULTS.md) file). Do not collate the two submissions.

@CiprianFlorin-Ifrim I didn't mean to imply your work built on mine, I know it wasn't. It was extremely good and I'm genuinely glad the ternary approach was pushed this far. Separately, I do wish my submissions had gotten reviewed.

> @CiprianFlorin-Ifrim I didn't mean to imply your work built on mine, I know it wasn't. It was extremely good and I'm genuinely glad the ternary approach was pushed this far. Separately, I do wish my submissions had gotten reviewed.

All love, it could be that the title gave the wrong idea? I see people using Record for the main leaderboard and non-record for the "I am uploading something now to have a PR here, but will update it later", which imo even adds pressure on the organisers to manager the hundreds of them. Mine had the specific leaderboard name, "notable non record runs". Also the time too, as it looks like they want the notable non record leaderboard to be just for weird stuff with unlimited compute.
